text.title: STAR Method: Components, Flow, and Follow-Up Probes
text.summary: STAR component definitions, the four-step interview flow, follow-up probe questions, and patterns for distinguishing complete from incomplete responses.

rule.statement: A complete STAR response requires all four components; accept nothing as evidence until the Action and Result are specific and attributable to the candidate personally.
rule.rationale: Candidates default to team-credit framing ("we delivered...") and omit results because they are uncertain what to share. Probing for personal contribution and quantified outcomes converts vague stories into scorable evidence.

section.title: Component Definitions
section.p: Situation: the context or background — who, what, when, what was at stake. The interviewer prompt: "Tell me about a time when..."
Task: the candidate's specific responsibility in the situation — not the team's goal, the candidate's goal. Probe: "What was your role specifically?"
Action: what the candidate specifically did — concrete steps in first-person singular. Probe: "Walk me through exactly what YOU did, not what the team did."
Result: the outcome and its scope — quantified where possible, time-bounded, learning noted. Probe: "What was the outcome? What changed as a result? What would you do differently?"

rule.statement: Follow the four-step flow for every behavioral question: ask, listen for the Situation, probe for missing Task and Action specificity, confirm the Result.
rule.rationale: Skipping probes when the story sounds good is the primary source of false positives. Compelling narratives often lack the candidate's personal contribution — probing exposes whether the role was central or peripheral.

list.item: Step 1: Ask the behavioral question. Let the candidate answer without interruption.
Step 2: Listen for Situation. Note: is the context specific? Is there a timeframe? Who was involved?
Step 3: Probe for Task and Action. Ask: "What was your specific responsibility?" and "Walk me through exactly what you did." Distinguish personal action from team action.
Step 4: Confirm the Result. Ask: "What was the outcome?" and "What metrics can you share?" and "What did you learn?" If the result is vague, ask: "What would have happened if you hadn't done that?"

section.title: Clarification Probes
section.p: "Can you be more specific about [the situation / the decision / the outcome]?"
"When did this happen? How long did it take?"

section.title: Personal-Contribution Probes
section.p: "What was YOUR contribution versus the team's?"
"If you hadn't been involved, what would have happened?"
"Who else was involved? What did they do specifically?"

section.title: Quantification Probes
section.p: "What were the specific numbers or metrics?"
"How does that compare to before you made the change?"

section.title: Learning Probes
section.p: "What would you do differently if you faced this again?"
"What did you learn from this experience?"

example.note: Complete STAR: "When the migration project fell 3 weeks behind (S), I was responsible for unblocking the backend team's dependency on the data model changes (T). I held daily standups with the data team, reprioritized 4 schema changes to unblock the critical path, and shipped a compatibility shim that let both teams work in parallel (A). We recovered 2 of the 3 weeks and launched within 1 week of the original date; the shim became a pattern two other teams adopted (R)."

Missing Action: "The project was falling behind, so our team fixed the dependency issues and we hit the deadline." No personal contribution visible; probe required: "What specifically did you do to fix the dependency issues?"

Missing Result: "I led a cross-functional team and we worked really hard on the migration." No outcome; probe required: "What was the result of the migration? Did you hit the deadline? What changed for the users?"

Hypothetical answer: "I would approach a situation like that by first aligning stakeholders, then setting up a project plan..." The question asked for a past example. Probe: "Can you give me a specific situation where you actually did this?"
